subject: Physics
q:
The initial mass of a rocket is 1000 kg. Calculate at what rate the fuel should be burnt so that the rocket is given an acceleration of, 20 m s-2. The gases come out at a relative speed of 500 m s-1, with respect to the rocket:

Use g=10 m s-2
question:
The initial mass of a rocket is 1000 kg. Calculate at what rate the fuel should be burnt so that the rocket is given an acceleration of, 20 m s-2. The gases come out at a relative speed of 500 m s-1, with respect to the rocket:

Use g=10 m s-2
solution:
Here we are applying the equation, Fr−mg=ma

From Newton's second law, Fr=dmdtvr⇒dmdtvr=mg+ma

Put the given values in the above equations, 

⇒dmdt500=1000(10+20)⇒dmdt=60 kg s-1.

q: As shown in figure, a 70 kg garden roller is pushed with a force of F→=200 N at an angle of 30° with horizontal. The normal reaction on the roller is (Given g=10 m s-2)
question: As shown in figure, a 70 kg garden roller is pushed with a force of F→=200 N at an angle of 30° with horizontal. The normal reaction on the roller is (Given g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Component of the force perpendicular to the ground,

Fsin30°.

Now for equilibrium along vertical direction, we get

N=mg+Fsin30°=70×10+200×12=800 N

question: A block is kept on a frictionless inclined surface with angle of inclination $\alpha$. The incline is given an acceleration a to keep the block stationary. Then a is equal to

The air escapes from the balloon at a uniform rate with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Then, the average force acting on that balloon will be (in dyne).
question: A balloon has mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> in air. The air escapes from the balloon at a uniform rate with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the balloon shrinks in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> completely. Then, the average force acting on that balloon will be (in dyne).
solution:
Given that the mass of the air m=10 g and it escapes in time t=5 s with velocity v=4.5 cm s-1.

The average force on the balloon can be given as,

F=vdmdt

⇒F=4.5×105=9 dyne

subject: Physics
q: A conical pendulum of length l makes an angle θ=45° with respect to Z-axis and moves in a circle in the XY plane. The radius of the circle is 0.4 m and its center is vertically below O. The speed of the pendulum, in its circular path, will be - Take g=10 m s-2
question: A conical pendulum of length l makes an angle θ=45° with respect to Z-axis and moves in a circle in the XY plane. The radius of the circle is 0.4 m and its center is vertically below O. The speed of the pendulum, in its circular path, will be - Take g=10 m s-2
solution:
Tsin⁡θ=mv2r

Tcos⁡θ=mg

tan⁡θ=v2rg

θ=45o

v2=rg

v=rg=0.4×10

v=2 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
A hanging mass M is connected to a four times bigger mass by using a string pulley arrangement, as shown in the figure. The bigger mass is placed on a horizontal ice-slab and being pulled by 2Mg force. In this situation, tension in the string is x5Mg for x=_____. Neglect mass of the string and friction of the block (bigger mass) with ice slab.
(Given g= acceleration due to gravity)
question:
A hanging mass M is connected to a four times bigger mass by using a string pulley arrangement, as shown in the figure. The bigger mass is placed on a horizontal ice-slab and being pulled by 2Mg force. In this situation, tension in the string is x5Mg for x=_____. Neglect mass of the string and friction of the block (bigger mass) with ice slab.
(Given g= acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
Equation for 4M: 2Mg-T=4Ma   ...1

Equation for M: T-Mg=Ma   ...2

Adding both equation we get,

Mg=5Ma ⇒a=g5

Now, tension T=Mg+Ma=6Mg5

Therefore, x=6

solution:

$\therefore \quad a=g \sin \theta$
where $\mathrm{a}$ is along the inclined plane
$\therefore \quad$ vertical component of acceleration is $\mathrm{g} \sin ^2 \theta$
$\therefore \quad$ relative vertical acceleration of $A$ with respect to $B$ is

question: A cricket player catches a ball of mass 120 g moving with 25 m s-1 speed. If the catching process is completed in 0.1 s then the magnitude of force exerted by the ball on the hand of player will be(in SI unit):
solution:
Average force exerted will be, Fav=ΔpΔt

subject: Physics
q: Consider a uniform cubical box of side a on a rough floor that is to be moved by applying minimum possible force F at a point b above its centre of mass (see figure). If the coefficient of friction is μ=0.4 , the maximum possible value of 100×ba for a box not to topple before moving is ________
question: Consider a uniform cubical box of side a on a rough floor that is to be moved by applying minimum possible force F at a point b above its centre of mass (see figure). If the coefficient of friction is μ=0.4 , the maximum possible value of 100×ba for a box not to topple before moving is ________
solution:
For no toppling
Fa2+b≤mga2
μa2+μb≤a2
0.2a+0.4b≤0.5a
0.4b≤0.3a
b≤3a2
b≤0.75a (in limiting case)
But it is not possible as b can maximum be equal to 0.5a
∴100bamax=50.00

subject: Physics
q: A $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ brick begins to slide over a surface which is inclined at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with respect to horizontal axis. The co-efficient of static friction between their surfaces is:
question: A $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ brick begins to slide over a surface which is inclined at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with respect to horizontal axis. The co-efficient of static friction between their surfaces is:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{mg} \sin 45=\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{L}} \\ & \mathrm{mg} \cos 45=\mathrm{N} \\ & \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mu_{\mathrm{s}} N \\ & \mu_{\mathrm{s}}=\tan 45=1 \end{aligned}$ or $\tan \theta=\mu_s$ ( $\theta$ is angle of repose) $\tan 45=\mu_{\mathrm{s}}=1$

subject: Physics
q: A block of 3 kg is attached to a string whose other end is attached to the wall. An unknown force F is applied so that the string makes an angle of 30° with the wall. The tension T is : (Given g =10 m s–2)
question: A block of 3 kg is attached to a string whose other end is attached to the wall. An unknown force F is applied so that the string makes an angle of 30° with the wall. The tension T is : (Given g =10 m s–2)
solution:
From geometry, θ=30°.

For equilibrium,

Tcosθ=mg

⇒T=mgcos30°⇒T=3×1032

⇒T=20 N

subject: Physics
q: A cubic block of mass $m$ is sliding down on an inclined plane at $60^{\circ}$ with an acceleration of $\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}$, the value of coefficient of kinetic friction is
question: A cubic block of mass $m$ is sliding down on an inclined plane at $60^{\circ}$ with an acceleration of $\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}$, the value of coefficient of kinetic friction is
solution:
$\mathrm{mg} \sin 60^{\circ}-\mu \mathrm{mg} \cos 60^{\circ}=\mathrm{ma}$
$g \sin 60-\mu g \cos 60=\frac{g}{2}$
$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{\mu}{2}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\mu=\sqrt{3}-1$

subject: Physics
q: A particle is moving with uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> under the action of a central fictitious force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> which is inversely proportional to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>. Its time period of revolution will be given by :
question: A particle is moving with uniform speed along the circumference of a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> under the action of a central fictitious force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> which is inversely proportional to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>R</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>. Its time period of revolution will be given by :
solution:
F∝1R3

KR3=mω2R

ω2=Km×1R4

2πT2=Km×1R4

T2∝R4

T∝R2

subject: Physics
q:
In the given figure the blocks $A, B$ and $C$ weigh $4 \mathrm{~kg}, 6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 8 kg respectively. The co-efficient of sliding friction between any two surfaces is 0.5. The force $\vec{F}$ required to slide the block $C$ with constant speed is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
(Use $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question:
In the given figure the blocks $A, B$ and $C$ weigh $4 \mathrm{~kg}, 6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 8 kg respectively. The co-efficient of sliding friction between any two surfaces is 0.5. The force $\vec{F}$ required to slide the block $C$ with constant speed is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
(Use $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
For the 8 kg block (C) to move with constant velocity, $F_{net} = 0$.

Friction forces acting:
- Between C and ground: $f = \mu(m_A + m_B + m_C)g = 0.5 \times (4+6+8) \times 10 = 90$ N
- Between C and B: $f = \mu(m_A + m_B)g = 0.5 \times (4+6) \times 10 = 50$ N

For the 6 kg block (B):
$T = f_{\text{B on ground}} + f_{\text{A on B}}$
$T = \mu(m_A + m_B)g \cdot \frac{m_B}{m_A+m_B} + \mu \cdot m_A \cdot g$
$T = 20 + 50 = 70$ N

For the 8 kg block (C):
$F = 90 + T + 50 = 90 + 70 + 50 = 210$ N

subject: Physics
q: A car of mass $1000 \mathrm{~kg}$ is moving at a speed of 30 $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. Brakes are applied to bring the car to rest. If the net retarding force is $5000 \mathrm{~N}$, the car comes to stop after travelling $d \mathrm{~m}$ in $t \mathrm{~s}$. Then
question: A car of mass $1000 \mathrm{~kg}$ is moving at a speed of 30 $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. Brakes are applied to bring the car to rest. If the net retarding force is $5000 \mathrm{~N}$, the car comes to stop after travelling $d \mathrm{~m}$ in $t \mathrm{~s}$. Then
solution:
Given: mass of car $m=1000 \mathrm{~kg}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& u=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& v=0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \text { retarding force } f=5000 \mathrm{~N} \\
& \therefore \text { retardation, }-a=\frac{5000}{1000}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$
By equation, $v^2-u^2=2$ as
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 0-(30)^2=-2 \times 5 \times d \\
& \therefore d=\frac{900}{10}=90 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}
$$
and $a=\frac{v-u}{t} \therefore t=\frac{v-u}{a}=\frac{0-30}{-5}=6 \mathrm{~s}$

subject: Physics
q: A bullet of mass $4 \mathrm{~g}$ is fired horizontally with a speed of $300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ into $0.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ block of wood at rest on a table. If the coefficient of friction between the block and the table is $0.3$, how far will the block slide approximately?
question: A bullet of mass $4 \mathrm{~g}$ is fired horizontally with a speed of $300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ into $0.8 \mathrm{~kg}$ block of wood at rest on a table. If the coefficient of friction between the block and the table is $0.3$, how far will the block slide approximately?
solution:
Given, $\mathrm{m}_1=4 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{u}_1=300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ $\mathrm{m}_2=0.8 \mathrm{~kg}=800 \mathrm{~g}, \mathrm{u}_2=0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ From law of conservation of momentum, $\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{u}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{u}_2=\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2$ Let the velocity of combined system $=\mathrm{v} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ then, $4 \times 300+800 \times 0=(800+4) \times v$ $\mathrm{v}=\frac{1200}{804}=1.49 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Now, $\mu=0.3$ (given)
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\mathrm{a}=\mu \mathrm{g} \\
&\mathrm{a}=0.3 \times 10\left(\text { take } \mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right) \\
&=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$
then, from $\mathrm{v}^2=\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{as}$
$$
(1.49)^2=0+2 \times 3 \times s
$$

$\mathrm{s}=\frac{\left(1.49^2\right)}{6} ; \mathrm{s}=\frac{2.22}{6}=0.379 \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q: The normal reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> for a vehicle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> mass, negotiating a turn on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> banked road at maximum possible speed without skidding is ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: The normal reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> for a vehicle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> mass, negotiating a turn on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> banked road at maximum possible speed without skidding is ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
At vmax, f will be limiting in nature.

∴ Balancing force in vertical direction, Ncos30°-mg-μNcos60°=0
⇒Ncos30°-μcos60°=mg
∴ N=800×10(0.87-0.1)≈10.2×103 kg m s-2
Hence, option 1.

subject: Physics

Then the maximum horizontal force that can be applied on the larger block so that the blocks move together is N. (Round off to the Nearest Integer) [Take g as 9.8 m s-2]
question: Two blocks (m=0.5 kg and M=4.5 kg) are arranged on a horizontal frictionless table as shown in the figure. The coefficient of static friction between the two blocks is 37. Then the maximum horizontal force that can be applied on the larger block so that the blocks move together is N. (Round off to the Nearest Integer) [Take g as 9.8 m s-2]
solution:
amax=μg=37×9.8

F=(M+m)amax=5amax

=21 N

subject: Physics
q: The minimum velocity (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ ) with which a car driver must traverse a flat curve of radius $150 \mathrm{~m}$ and coefficient of friction $0.6$ to avoid skidding is
question: The minimum velocity (in $\mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ ) with which a car driver must traverse a flat curve of radius $150 \mathrm{~m}$ and coefficient of friction $0.6$ to avoid skidding is
solution: The condition to avoid skidding, $\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\mu \mathrm{rg}}=\sqrt{0.6 \times 150 \times 10}=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: Two masses A and B, each of mass M are fixed together by a massless spring, A force acts on the mass B as shown in figure. If the mass A starts moving away from mass B with acceleration a, then the acceleration of mass B will be :
question: Two masses A and B, each of mass M are fixed together by a massless spring, A force acts on the mass B as shown in figure. If the mass A starts moving away from mass B with acceleration a, then the acceleration of mass B will be :
solution:
acm=m1a1+m2a2m1+m2

F2M=Ma+MaB2M

aB=F-MaM

subject: Physics
q: A car is moving on a horizontal curved road with radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The approximate maximum speed of car will be, if friction between tyres and road is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn></math>. [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A car is moving on a horizontal curved road with radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The approximate maximum speed of car will be, if friction between tyres and road is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>34</mn></math>. [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
For a car moving with uniform speed along a circular track, the centripetal acceleration is provided by the frictional force acting radially inwards. Therefore, applying Newton's second law along radial direction,

μmg=mv2r

⇒v=μrg

   =0.34×50×10

    ≈13 m s-1

q: A spring whose unstrentches length is <math><mi>l</mi></math> has a force constant <math><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The spring is cut into two pieces of unstretches lengths <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> where, <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>n</mi><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mi>n</mi></math> is an integer. The ratio <math><mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mrow><mi> </mi></math> of the corresponding force constants, <math><msub><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> will be:
question: A spring whose unstrentches length is <math><mi>l</mi></math> has a force constant <math><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The spring is cut into two pieces of unstretches lengths <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> where, <math><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>n</mi><msub><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mi>n</mi></math> is an integer. The ratio <math><mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mrow><mi> </mi></math> of the corresponding force constants, <math><msub><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> will be:
solution:
Spring constants of the spring will be inversely proportional to length.
k1k2=l2l1=1n

subject: Physics
q:
A steel block of 10 kg rests on a horizontal floor as shown. When three iron cylinders are placed on it as shown, the block and cylinders go down with an acceleration 0.2 m s-2. The normal reaction R' by the floor if mass of the iron cylinders are equal and of 20 kg each is in N,

[Take g=10 m s-2 and μs=0.2
question:
A steel block of 10 kg rests on a horizontal floor as shown. When three iron cylinders are placed on it as shown, the block and cylinders go down with an acceleration 0.2 m s-2. The normal reaction R' by the floor if mass of the iron cylinders are equal and of 20 kg each is in N,

[Take g=10 m s-2 and μs=0.2
solution:
Writing force equation in vertical direction, 
Mg-N=Ma
⇒70 g-N=70×0.2
⇒N=70[ g-0.2]=70×9.8
∴N=686 newton
Note: Since there is no compressive normal from the sides, hence friction will not act.
Hence option 2.

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> experiences two forces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> The acceleration acting on the body is:
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> experiences two forces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>.</mo></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> The acceleration acting on the body is:
solution:
Net force F→=F→1+F→2=8i^+4j^+4k^

Therefore, required acceleration a→=F→m=2i^+j^+k^

subject: Physics
q: A block takes $t$ time to slide down a plane inclined at $45°$ to the horizontal. If the surface is made smooth (frictionless), the block takes time $\dfrac{t}{2}$ to slide down the plane. The coefficient of friction between the block and the inclined plane is $\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{100}\right)$. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
question: A block takes $t$ time to slide down a plane inclined at $45°$ to the horizontal. If the surface is made smooth (frictionless), the block takes time $\dfrac{t}{2}$ to slide down the plane. The coefficient of friction between the block and the inclined plane is $\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{100}\right)$. The value of $\alpha$ is __________.
solution:
Let the length of the inclined plane be $s$.

For the smooth inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_1 = g \sin \theta$.

The time taken is $t_1 = \dfrac{t}{2}$. Using the second equation of motion $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$ with $u = 0$:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} (g \sin \theta) \left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} g \sin \theta t^2$

For the rough inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_2 = g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta)$.

The time taken is $t_2 = t$. Using the equation of motion again:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta) t^2$

Equating the two expressions for $s$:

$\dfrac{1}{8} g \sin \theta t^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta) t^2$

$\dfrac{1}{4} \sin \theta = \sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta$

Given $\theta = 45^{\circ}$, we have $\sin 45^{\circ} = \cos 45^{\circ} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. Substituting these values:

$\dfrac{1}{4} = 1 - \mu$

$\mu = 1 - \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75$

We are given $\mu = \dfrac{\alpha}{100}$.

$\dfrac{\alpha}{100} = 0.75 \Rightarrow \alpha = 75$

Answer: $75$

subject: Physics
q: At $t=0$, a body of mass $100$ g starts moving under the influence of a force $(5\hat{i}+10\hat{j})$ N. After $2$ s its position is $(2x\hat{i}+5y\hat{j})$ m. The ratio $x:y$ is ______.
question: At $t=0$, a body of mass $100$ g starts moving under the influence of a force $(5\hat{i}+10\hat{j})$ N. After $2$ s its position is $(2x\hat{i}+5y\hat{j})$ m. The ratio $x:y$ is ______.
solution:
Mass $m = 100 \text{ g} = 0.1 \text{ kg}$

Force $\vec{F} = 5\hat{i} + 10\hat{j} \text{ N}$

Acceleration $\vec{a} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{m} = \dfrac{5\hat{i} + 10\hat{j}}{0.1} = 50\hat{i} + 100\hat{j} \text{ m/s}^2$

Since the body starts from rest, initial velocity $\vec{u} = 0$.

Position after $t = 2 \text{ s}$ is given by $\vec{r} = \vec{u}t + \dfrac{1}{2}\vec{a}t^2$

$\vec{r} = \dfrac{1}{2}(50\hat{i} + 100\hat{j})(2)^2 = 100\hat{i} + 200\hat{j} \text{ m}$

Comparing with the given position $\vec{r} = 2x\hat{i} + 5y\hat{j}$:

$2x = 100 \Rightarrow x = 50$

$5y = 200 \Rightarrow y = 40$

The ratio $x:y = 50:40 = 5:4$

Answer: $5:4$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement-I: An elevator can go up or down with uniform speed when its weight is balanced with the tension of its cable.
Statement-II: Force exerted by the floor of an elevator on the foot of a person standing on it is more than his/her weight when the elevator goes down with increasing speed.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement-I: An elevator can go up or down with uniform speed when its weight is balanced with the tension of its cable.
Statement-II: Force exerted by the floor of an elevator on the foot of a person standing on it is more than his/her weight when the elevator goes down with increasing speed.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
When elevator is moving with uniform speed T=Fg, as the net force along the vertical direction should be zero.

Therefore, Statement-I is true.

Now, when elevator is going down with increasing speed, its acceleration is downward.
Hence, 

W-N=ma⇒W-N=Wg×a

⇒N=W1-ag

Clearly, normal reaction is less than weight. So Statement-II is false.

subject: Physics
q: A body of weight $200 \mathrm{~N}$ is suspended from a tree branch through a chain of mass $10 \mathrm{~kg}$. The branch pulls the chain by a force equal to (if $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ) :
question: A body of weight $200 \mathrm{~N}$ is suspended from a tree branch through a chain of mass $10 \mathrm{~kg}$. The branch pulls the chain by a force equal to (if $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ ) :
solution: Chain block system is in equilibrium so $\mathrm{T}=200+100=300 \mathrm{~N} \text {. }$
